title: Penne al salmone
subtitle: Penne with smoked salmon and cream
excerpt: Penne tossed with strips of smoked salmon in a light cream and shallot sauce with a splash of vodka and dill. Italy's most popular fish pasta outside the coasts — a quick, rich weeknight dish that requires nothing more than pantry ingredients.
sourceNotes: Based on the Italian penne al salmone tradition as documented in Giorgio Locatelli, 'Made in Italy' (2006) and Ursula Ferrigno, 'Ursula Ferrigno's Complete Italian Cookbook' (2004). Penne al salmone became widespread across Italy from the 1980s as smoked salmon became more available and affordable.

content.text: Penne al salmone is a dish of the 1980s Italian cucina moderna — quicker and lighter than classical preparations, using ingredients that would have been unusual before the decade's supermarket expansion. Smoked salmon was expensive and rare in Italy before refrigerated transport made it widely available; by the mid-1980s it was standard in northern Italian home cooking. The sauce is simple: softened shallot, a splash of vodka flamed off, cream reduced briefly, salmon strips added at the last moment so they warm through without cooking.

The salmon should not be cooked — it goes into the sauce off the heat and the residual warmth is sufficient. Cooked smoked salmon becomes tough and loses its silky texture. The dill is added at the end for the same reason.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a wide frying pan over a medium heat. Add {{shallots}} diced shallots and cook for 5 minutes until soft and translucent. Pour in {{double-cream}} of double cream and bring to a gentle simmer. Cook for 3 minutes until slightly thickened. Add {{lemon}} of lemon juice and {{black-pepper-ground}} of pepper. Remove from the heat.

Cook {{penne}} of penne in well-salted boiling water until al dente. Reserve a cup of pasta water, then drain. Add the drained pasta to the cream sauce and toss over a low heat with a splash of pasta water if needed. Remove from the heat. Fold {{smoked-salmon}} of smoked salmon strips gently through the pasta — the residual heat is enough to warm them without cooking. Scatter {{dill}} fresh dill over and serve immediately.

items.symptom: Smoked salmon is tough and rubbery
items.cause: Added while the sauce was still on the heat
items.fix: Remove the pan from the heat before folding in the salmon. It needs only to warm through, not to cook.

items.cause: Double cream too much for the quantity
items.fix: Use 150 ml cream and add pasta water to reach the right consistency. The sauce should coat the pasta lightly, not sit in a pool.

content.text: Penne al salmone is northern Italian urban cooking of the 1980s — the decade when Italian food became simultaneously more cosmopolitan and more convenience-driven. It appears on the menus of Italian restaurants across Britain from the mid-1990s onwards, where it occupied the position of reliable crowd-pleaser: recognisably Italian, mild in flavour, and built from ingredients that could be held in the fridge for a week.
